statusLine: EU fuel security is under strain with 15 of 27 countries showing critical stock levels in at least one fuel type, while oil prices surge above $105 per barrel.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security faces significant pressure as 15 of 27 EU countries report critical stock levels in at least one fuel category, despite EU-wide averages remaining above the 90-day mandatory minimum. Romania presents the most concerning situation with all three fuel types in critical status - petrol at just 33.2 days, diesel at 32.4 days, and jet fuel at 76.4 days. Poland, Luxembourg, and Germany also show critical levels across multiple fuel categories, with Poland's jet fuel reserves particularly alarming at only 21.3 days.

Jet fuel presents the most widespread vulnerability, with 19 of 27 countries reporting critical stock levels below 90 days. This aviation fuel shortage affects major economies including Germany (61.4 days), France (59.6 days), and the Netherlands (59 days). Diesel stocks show more variation, with 13 countries in critical status while others like the Netherlands (215.7 days) and Finland (247 days) maintain substantial reserves.

The supply pressure coincides with Brent crude oil surging to $105.22 per barrel, up 7.02% and approximately 27% higher since the current Middle East conflict began. This has translated into elevated fuel prices across Europe, with petrol averaging €1.764 per liter and diesel at €1.943 per liter. The Netherlands and Denmark face the highest petrol costs at over €2.20 per liter, while Malta and Poland offer some relief with prices below €1.45 per liter.
keyPoints: 15 of 27 EU countries report critical stock levels in at least one fuel type, with Romania showing the most severe shortages across all categories
Jet fuel presents the greatest vulnerability with 19 of 27 countries below 90-day reserves, including major economies like Germany and France
Brent crude oil has surged 27% since the Middle East conflict began, now trading at $105.22 per barrel
Despite individual country pressures, EU-wide averages remain above mandatory minimums: petrol at 98.9 days, diesel at 97.8 days
Fuel price disparities are significant across the bloc, with Netherlands petrol costs 70% higher than Malta's
